comments.text: This does not resolve the question because Thomassen's paper investigates properties of graphs with uncountable chromatic number, while the present question asks to prove *existence* of graphs with uncountable chromatic number (and other properties).

Should that be $\leq\aleph_0$ at the end of both questions? Note that in his 1969 paper Erdős uses the same wording as on this page, but the original source, Problem 2 of the 1968 paper there is a $\leq$ appearing. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

It's unclear - as you say, the two sources differ, and I'm not close enough to this area to say which is the more sensible question. I'll add a note pointing out the ambiguity, and hopefully an expert can weigh in at some point.

If he really means "subgraph" as opposed to "induced subgraph," then it must be $\le \aleph_0$ because otherwise you could just take any set of $\aleph_1$ many vertices with no edges as a subgraph with chromatic number 1. So I would guess that $\le \aleph_0$ is the correct interpretation. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Even if “induced subgraph” is intended then a similar argument for a negative answer was discovered by AlphaProof as follows. Assume $(V, E)$ is such a graph. If $V’\subseteq V$ is any subset of vertices of size $\aleph_1$, then it has a coloring $c$ on $\aleph_0$ colors. By a pigeonhole principle argument, there’s a fiber $V_y = \{v \in V’ | c(v) = y\}$ of $c$ size $\aleph_1$. The induced subgraph $G[V_y]$ then has chromatic number $1$, but by assumption it has chromatic number $\aleph_0$. So indeed I think it must be $\leq\aleph_0$ in the statement.
